"

### Depth of Content and Clarity of Explanations

### Use of Good Practices

### Improvement of Content

- [x] Substantive changes to layout
- [x] Improvement of content
- [x] Improvement of layout 
 Aberrant neurogenesis in the olfactory epithelium of mice lacking the neurogenic receptor GFR alpha1.
A key event in the formation of the olfactory system is the generation of olfactory sensory neurons (OSNs) from stem cells in the olfactory epithelium (OE). GDNF and its receptor, GFR alpha1, are expressed in the basal cells of the OE, suggesting a role during OSN neurogenesis. To test this hypothesis, we have analyzed the phenotype of mice constitutively lacking GFR alpha1. GFR alpha1(-/-) mice exhibit a striking reduction of the OE, and a complete absence of OSNs, but no other defects in the development of the olfactory system. GFR alpha1(-/-) mice die perinatally, probably due to an impaired respiratory drive, and the females are infertile. The olfactory bulb of GFR alpha1(-/-) mice is smaller than that of wild-type mice, but neonatal OSN axons reach the glomeruli. We conclude that GDNF and GFR alpha1 play an essential role in OSN neurogenesis and in the generation of new OSNs, but their function is not required in the generation of OSN axons. A Miniature Soft Printed
